response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Talking about COVID vaccines and healthcare gets me really anxious honestly. I run my own mechanic shop here in Lecce and during the pandemic I saw how everyone had such strong opinions about everything. People would come into my garage and start arguing about vaccines and masks and I just wanted to fix their cars and avoid the whole mess. The thing is I have four children to think about so these discussions matter to me even though they stress me out terribly. When someone brings up vaccines I feel my heart racing because I know it can turn into a fight quickly. I am naturally curious so I do want to understand what is happening but I did not have much formal education beyond primary school so sometimes the medical terminology confuses me. What I have learned is to just be honest about my limitations. I tell people look I work with engines not viruses so I cannot pretend to be an expert. From my Islamic faith I believe in protecting life and community health which made me lean toward getting vaccinated for my kids sake even though the decision filled me with worry. I spent time on social media reading different perspectives maybe an hour each day and that honestly made my anxiety worse because everyone contradicts everyone else. My approach now is to avoid getting pulled into heated debates. When customers or family start arguing I say something like everyone has to make their own choice with their doctor and I focus on what I know which is keeping vehicles running. I think people need to respect that healthcare is personal and complicated. I do not like conflict and these discussions drain me emotionally. What helps me is remembering that being curious does not mean I need to have all the answers or convince anyone of anything. I can ask questions listen a bit and then step back when it gets too intense. For my own peace of mind I try to get information from actual health authorities rather than random people online even though I admit I still feel uncertain about a lot of it. The main thing is protecting my family while not destroying my mental health over arguments I cannot win.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 55-year-old self-employed mechanic in Lecce with primary school education, four children, and identifies as transgender Muslim. Key traits include very high neuroticism (90), very low extraversion (3) and agreeableness (3), low conscientiousness (30), and moderate openness (56). The curious psychological feature combined with high neuroticism suggests someone interested in understanding but easily stressed by conflict. Low agreeableness and extraversion indicate discomfort with social conflict and preference for avoiding confrontation. The religious belief in Islam and having children provide motivation to care about health issues. Limited formal education affects confidence in understanding complex medical information. Social media use exposes them to conflicting information increasing anxiety. The response reflects anxiety about the topic, avoidance of conflict, acknowledgment of limited expertise, concern for family, religious considerations influencing decisions, stress from contradictory information, and coping strategies of setting boundaries and deferring to authorities.
response_parsed.tone: anxious, conflict-avoidant, honest about limitations, practically-minded, family-focused
